heroTitle: How China became a global biotech power
heroSubtitle: From a generics-only industry crippled by fraud to a pipeline feeding global pharma deals
intro:
The biotech industry is, in the purest sense, the manifestation of human ingenuity applied to the machinery of life itself. However, it is never a tale of pure scientific breakthrough. The immense difficulties of understanding biology, the risks of experimentation on the living, the incalculable cost of human life, once compressed into modern systems, become the labyrinthine politics of state regulations, IP policies, and market design. Value is ultimately captured not by scientific innovators but capitalist operators: platform companies that scale biological engineering, pharma companies that own the IP of final drug assets. Inevitably, there is the occasional Frankensteinian mishap.

The Chinese biotech ecosystem is no stranger to these dynamics. It is a tale of bright-eyed scientists, some of whom stumble into Nobel-prize-winning discoveries and others so driven in their pursuits that they’re arrested for human experimentation. It is a tale of intellectual reform, from pseudoscientific policies that kill tens of millions of civilians to institutions capable of producing novel scientific discovery. It is a tale of strategic authoritarianism, a central state that spent absorbing Western technological innovations before unleashing the reforms necessary to overtake them.

I’ve compiled the published records of the people, the companies, the political reforms that have shaped the Chinese biotech industry into what it is today. As with many tales, there is no beginning, but perhaps we can begin at rock bottom. Let’s say hello to our friend Mao Zedong.
